Ser
Wallace Waynwood
Title Ser[1]
Allegiance House Waynwood
Culture Valemen
Born 278–283 AC[2]
Mother Anya Waynwood
Books
  • A Game of Thrones (appears)
  • A Feast for Crows (appendix)
  • A Dance with Dragons (appendix)
  • The Winds of Winter (appears)

Wallace Waynwood is the youngest son of Lady Anya Waynwood.[3][1]

Contents

  • 1 Appearance and Character
  • 2 Recent Events
    • 2.1 A Game of Thrones
    • 2.2 The Winds of Winter
  • 3 Quotes
  • 4 Family
  • 5 Notes
  • 6 References

Appearance and Character[ ]

Wallace is long-faced and lantern-jawed, and has stringy brown hair and a pinched nose. He talks with a stutter. Wallace is not as muscular or tall as his nephew, Ser Roland Waynwood. [1]

Recent Events[ ]

A Game of Thrones[ ]

Lady Anya Waynwood is present in the High Hall of the Eyrie, surrounded by her sons, when Tyrion Lannister demands a trial by battle to prove his innocence of the claims against him.[4]

The Winds of Winter[ ]

 Warning
This information has thus far been released in a sample chapter for The Winds of Winter, and might therefore not be in finalized form. Keep in mind that the content as described below is still subject to change.

Wallace is knighted in 300 AC.[N 1]

Ser Wallace arrives with his mother, Lady Anya, his nephew, Ser Roland Waynwood, and Ser Harrold Hardyng, at the Gates of the Moon for the tourney held to decide on the first eight members of the Brotherhood of Winged Knights. Upon arrival, Wallace is met by Alayne Stone and Myranda Royce. Wallace and Roland joke about each other, and Alayne courteously helps Wallace when the newly-made knight stutters.[1]

Alayne dances with Wallace at the feast that evening. Wallace stutters again when trying to comment on other guests, so Alayne takes over the conversation to spare him from embarrassment.[1]

Quotes[ ]

Roland: Then how do you explain this hole in my chest, from where you stole my heart?
Wallace: He is only t-teasing you, my lady. My n-n-nephew never had a h-h-heart.
Rolland: The Waynwood wheel has a broken spoke, and we have my nuncle here. Squires should be quiet when knights are speaking.
Wallace: I am no more a s-squire, my lady. My n-nephew knows full well that I was k-k-kni-k-k-kni—
Alayne: Dubbed?
Wallace: Dubbed.[1]

—Roland Waynwood, Wallace, and Alayne Stone
